heading: 4.5.2 Thinning algorithm (bounding technique)
text:
Thin introduction The thinning algorithm is almost identical to the exploitation of dominance relations in the algorithm design literature (Ibaraki, 1977; Bird and De Moor, 1996). Both thinning and classical dominance relations aim to improve the time complexity of naive dynamic programming algorithms (Bird and De Moor, 1996). The key difference is that thinning considers not only the theoretical effectiveness of bounds but also their practical implementation. This aspect is often overlooked in studies of branch-and-bound algorithms, where researchers focus on the existence alone of a bounding relation but neglect its efficiency. As noted in subSection 2.1, a bounding technique must be proven effective: the computational time it saves must outweigh the time required to execute it. If a bounding check is computationally expensive yet reduces the solution space only marginally, its application becomes ineffective.
The thinning technique leverages the fundamental observation that certain partial configurations are superior to others. Extending non-optimal partial configurations is thus a waste of computational resources. Formally, we define a function thin : ( A×A→ Bool ) × [ A ] → [ A ] which receives a relation R : A×A→ Bool , referred to as a dominance relation, and a sequence xs . The output is a subsequence ys ⊆ xs , satisfying:
In other words, for every element in the original sequence, there exists a dominating element in the thinned subsequence, ensuring that only potentially optimal configurations are retained for further computation. Properties (1) and (2) are known as the universal property of the thinning algorithm (Bird and De Moor, 1996).
Thinning is similar to, but distinct from, min E . Indeed, the min E function can be understood as a special case of thinning with respect to a total order defined by the objective function E . In contrast, thinning is based on a preorder R , because transitivity is required, and total orders are too restrictive for the purposes of thinning (He, 2025b; Bird and Gibbons, 2020). In a preorder relation, some configurations may be incomparable. Consequently, thin R : [ A ] → [ A ] receives a list and returns a list, whereas min E : [ A ] →A always returns a single element.
The thinning algorithm must satisfy the following laws:
such that R 1 ( a, b ) = ⇒ R 2 ( a, b ). The first law (50) is called thin introduction . It states that applying thinning does not change the optimal solution: the optimal solution after thinning is the same as the original optimal solution. The second law (51), called thin-map , governs the interaction between a map operation and thinning: mapping over a thinned sequence produces a result contained within the thinning of the mapped sequence.
Monotonicity and thinning Similar to monotonicity used in designing efficient combinatorial optimization algorithms, it also plays a crucial role in identifying valid dominance relations that enable thinning fusion. Intuitively, monotonicity ensures that if a partial configuration a dominates another configuration b , then extending both configurations in the same way preserves the dominance relation. Without this property, thinning could discard potentially optimal solutions.

In words, if configuration a dominates configuration b , then after applying the same update f to both, the dominance relation still holds.

Similarly, in sodt kperms , we have: R ( rs , rs ′ ) = ⇒ R ( insR ( r, rs ) , insR ( r, rs ′ )). Likewise, in sodt rec , the semantic interpretation of monotonicity in thinning can be expressed as R ( u, u ′ ) ∧ R ( v, v ′ ) ⊆ R ( DN ( u, r, v ) , DN ( u ′ , r, v ′ )).

For our purposes-the study of optimal decision tree problems-the dominance relation allows us to implement thin simply using the fi lter function. Therefore, we will not discuss the general construction of thinning, but instead focus on formalizing the specific dominance relations studied in ODT algorithms for machine learning research.
He (2025b) showed that dominance relations commonly used in ML can be abstracted into two types: finite dominance relation (FDR) and global upper bound (GUB). Both relations rely on two auxiliary functions defined as follows.

Assume smaller values are better: a function ub is called pessimistic upper bound if ub ( f n ( a )) ≤ ub ( a ) . A function lb : A×D→ R is called optimistic lower bound if lb ( f n ( a )) ≥ lb ( a ) .
Intuitively, these bounds capture the idea that as more information is accumulated, the bounds become tighter, assume small is better:
The pessimistic upper bound provides a 'worst-case' estimate of a , so any update f n ( a ) should yield a tighter (smaller) upper bound.
The optimistic lower bound provides a 'best-case' estimate of a , so any update f n ( a ) should yield a looser (larger) lower bound.

heading: 4.6.1 Comparison between size- and depth-constrained trees algorithms
text:
As noted, depth-constrained decision trees are commonly used in ODT studies with relatively small combinatorics, such as axis-parallel trees (Mazumder et al., 2022; Brita et al., 2025) trees over binary-feature data (Demirovic et al., 2022; Verwer and Zhang, 2019; Nijssen and Fromont, 2007; Aglin et al., 2020; Nijssen and Fromont, 2010). These existing methods typically process recursion in a depth-first manner, unlike the breadth-first formulation we defined in 37. In contrast, the size-constrained ODT algorithm has been far less studied; Hu et al. (2019); Lin et al. (2020); Zhang et al. (2023) have applied it to solve the ODT problem over binary-feature data, but the correctness of their algorithms remains unresolved.
This preference is not coincidental. The discussion below clarifies why researchers favor depth-constrained approaches and why the depth-constrained ODT algorithm 37 is unsuitable for problems with intractable combinatorics, such as hypersurface decision trees. The primary reason is that such problems require parallelization to achieve reasonable solution times, yet depth-constrained trees are difficult to implement in an embarrassingly parallel manner-parallelization requires minimal or no communication between processors.
We first analyze the size-constrained decision tree and explain why it is suitable for parallel implementation. In 26, the original size-constrained ODT problem can be factorized as:
This factorization indicates that solving the challenging odt problem can be achieved by solving many smaller instances of sodt . As He and Little (2025a) demonstrated, there exists an elegant and efficient divide-and-conquer definition for kcombs K , which decomposes the problem into smaller, independent subproblems. These subproblems can be processed in parallel and mapped efficiently onto modern memory hierarchies, particularly when combined with recursively blocked arrays (Gupta et al., 2000). Unfortunately, no similar factorization has yet been found for the depth-constrained ODT problem that would enable embarrassingly parallel execution. Consequently, the optimal decision tree algorithm derived from 24 can only be executed sequentially or parallelized post hoc, making it difficult to achieve the same level of speed-up as embarrassingly parallel algorithms.
For hyperplane decision trees, each recursive step may involve up to O ( N D ) possible rules. Adopting a breadth-first search strategy would result in extremely slow branching and high memory consumption. When exact solutions are infeasible-as is the case for most data lists with large d and N -depth-first search becomes necessary, as it can provide approximate solutions within a fixed time limit.
Although depth-first search effectively reduces memory usage, it significantly complicates parallelization compared to breadth-first strategies. Previous research on ODT problems with smaller combinatorics remains tractable even without parallelization because the number of possible splits at each recursive step is limited to O ( N × D ) for axis-parallel trees. However, this approach becomes ineffective when applied to the larger combinatorics of 24 where splitting rules are defined as hyperplanes. Hence, parallelization is likely the only viable solution for problems with such large combinatorial complexity.
Nevertheless, developing a combinatorial characterization for depth-constrained decision trees that allows parallel factorization, similar to the size-constrained ODT problem, represents an interesting direction for future research.

heading: 4.6.2 Comparison between size-constrained optimal decision tree algorithms
text:
Having discussed the advantages of depth- and size-constrained decision trees, we believe size-constrained ODT algorithms are more suitable candidates for solving complex ODT problems. We present three distinct definitions of odt size : odt rec , odt vec and odt kperms -naturally raising the question: which is most suitable?
In machine learning research, the interplay between software, hardware, and algorithms is often overlooked. An algorithm's success may derive not from its intrinsic superiority but from the tools-hardware and software-used in its implementation. This phenomenon, termed the 'hardware lottery' by Hooker (2021) highlights how the siloed development of hardware, software, and algorithms has historically shaped which approaches prevail. A prominent example is the success of deep neural networks, which benefited substantially from optimized hardware and software.
Similarly, in the ODT problem, a comparable 'lottery' exists between the vectorized method odt vec and recursive method odt rec for solving optimal decision tree problems. Because odt size can be factorized into many smaller instances solved using sodt , the algorithmic behavior is largely determined by the performance of sodt . Therefore, we focus our discussion on the behavior of these algorithms.
Compare with sodt rec , sodt vec and sodt kperms , sodt rec not only requires provably fewer computations due to its DP solution but also has a lower probability of incurring worstcase complexity. Due to the use of tree datatype, the recursive pattern of sodt rec behaves like a divide-and-conquer algorithm, so the combinatorial complexity of subtrees decreases logarithmically with respect to the length of rs in typical cases, whereas sodt vec decreases linearly due to its intrinsic sequential nature. Furthermore, sodt rec creates no duplicate trees, eliminating the need for a unique operation. However, its drawbacks are significant: it is difficult to optimize and carries a risk of stack overflow due to recursive calls. Moreover, sodt rec is a data-dependent recursion , in which the function's behavior and termination depend on the structure or values of the input, produces irregular and unpredictable branching and memory-access patterns that are challenging to parallelize efficiently.
In contrast, sodt vec is purely sequential and can be fully vectorized, offering substantial advantages. It enables incremental evaluation of the tree objective, as feasible splits are added recursively to leaves, requiring only simple leaf splits. Nevertheless, it is less efficient than sodt rec because no DP solution exists for the objective that we concerns, and additional computations arise from feasibility checks using the ancestry relation matrix K and the unique operation. This method is also more memory-intensive, particularly when processing data in batches. For instance, storing M trees with K splitting rules in a batch requires N × M × ( K +1) memory for predictions-a significant burden for large M and K . In practice, this can quickly exceed GPU memory limits.
The odt kperms algorithm faces similar challenges to sodt vec . Representing decision trees via K -permutations produces duplicate trees. Unlike sodt vec , duplicates can sometimes be removed using a unique operation, but permutations such as [1 , 2 , 3] and [1 , 3 , 2] correspond to the same tree require reconstructing the tree structure first, which incurs additional computational cost.
Overall, we believe sodt rec and sodt vec are the most suitable methods for solving the HODT problem. However, it remains unclear whether the hardware advantages of sodt vec will outweigh the computational efficiency of sodt rec . We summarized advantages and limitations of odt size algorithms in Table 1.
Additionally, Table 2 ranks odt algorithmsodt rec , odt vec , odt kperms , and odt depth -based on theoretical computational efficiency , memory usage , parallelizability , and recursive evaluability . Notably, odt kperms receives the lowest recursive evaluability rating of 5 because adding a new rule, such as inserting r 3 at the front of [ r 1 , r 2 ] may invalidates previously computed results, as the new tree structure may place r 1 and r 2 on different branches of r 3 .
In Part II, we implement optimized versions of sodt rec and sodt vec using more efficient array-based tree representations instead of tree structures. Experimental results align with expectations: although sodt rec is theoretically more efficient sodt vec outperforms it in both sequential and parallel settings due to better hardware compatibility. Future work may explore hardware tailored to sodt rec , enabling a more balanced comparison between these approaches.

heading: 5 Geometric foundation
text:
Having developed a comprehensive algorithmic theory for ODT problems, along with four distinct solutions for both size- and depth-constrained cases, we note that these theories rely on the assumption that the splitting rules ( rs ) or the method for generating these rules ( gen splits ) are known. However, in machine learning, such rules are typically not readily available; they must be inferred from data. Moreover, our theory is grounded in the axioms of the proper decision tree, but not all splitting rules satisfy these axioms-for example, those used in ODT problems over binary feature data.
In Section 5.1, we present a detailed analysis of the combinatorial and geometric aspects of hypersurfaces by drawing on results from the theory of hyperplane arrangements and algebraic geometry . We show that, when data points are in general position, hypersurfaces defined by fi xed-degree polynomials can be represented as combinations of data items and can be exhaustively generated in polynomial time. Furthermore, we demonstrate that such hypersurfaces satisfy the axioms of the proper decision tree.
Section 5.1 explains the construction of two splitting rule generators, that is, a concrete implementation of gen splits for hypersurface and mixed splitting rules . Finally, Subsection 5.3 presents a detailed combinatorial analysis of the complexity of various ODT problems.

heading: 5.1 Classification meet combinatorial geometry
text:
Polynomial hypersurfaces, and their special case-hyperplanes-are natural candidates for the splitting rules of a proper decision tree, as they naturally satisfy Axioms 1, 2, and 3. However, for Axiom 4, a subtle question arises: why can't the relations h i M ↘ h j M , h i M ↙ h j M coexist? At first glance, by B´ ezout's theorem, any two polynomial hypersurfaces must intersect at some points. Intuitively, this suggests that a hypersurface could lie on both sides of another by being 'cut' into different regions along their intersection points, thus both h i M ↘ h j M and h i M ↙ h j M should coexist because h i can cut h j and h j can cut h i into two parts. While this is true in general, the situation is different in the context of machine learning, where we are primarily concerned with the partition of data induced by hypersurfaces rather than the hypersurfaces themselves.
By enforcing a natural assumption on the data distribution, we show that the hypersurfaces generated from data can only lie on one side of another hypersurface, ensuring that the coexistence of h i M ↘ h j M and h i M ↙ h j M is impossible.

heading: 5.1.1 Polynomial hypersurface and the W -tuple Veronese embedding
text:
A monomial M with respect to a D -tuple x = ( x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ) is a product of the form M ( x ) = x α 1 1 · x α 2 2 . . . · x α D D , where α 1 , α 2 . . . , α D are non-negative integers, denote α = ( α 1 , α 2 . . . , α D ). The total degree of this monomial is the sum | α | = α 1 + · · · + α n .
Definition 21 Polynomial . A polynomial P ( x ) , or P in short, is a finite linear combination (with coefficients in the field R ) of monomials in the form
We denote the set of all polynomials with variables x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D and coefficients in R is denoted by R [ x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ] or R [ x ] .
The maximal degree of P , denoted deg ( P ), is the maximum | α i | such that the coefficient α j is nonzero. For instance, the polynomial P ( x ) = 5 x 2 1 + 3 x 1 x 2 + x 2 2 + x 1 + x 2 + 3 for x ∈ R 2 has six terms and maximal degree two.
The number of possible monomial terms of a degree K polynomial is equivalent to the number of ways of selecting K variables from the multisets of D +1 variables 3 . This is equivalent to the size K combinations of D + 1 elements taken with replacement . In other words, selecting K variables from the variable set ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x D ) in homogeneous coordinates with repetition. This lead to the following fact.
Fact 1 If polynomial P in R [ x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ] has maximal degree M , then polynomial P has ( D + M D ) monomial terms (including constant term) at most.
Given polynomial P ∈ R [ x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ] of degree M , a hypersurface h M defined by P is the set of zeros of P is h M = { ( x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ) ∈ R D : P ( x 1 , x 2 . . . , x D ) = 0 } . Geometrically, the zero set of a polynomial P determines a subset of R D . This subset is called a surface if its dimension is less than D and a hypersurface if its dimension is D -1. Specifically, if this subset is an affine subspace defined by a degree-one polynomial, it is referred to as a flat . A D -1-dimensional affine flat is known as a hyperplane .

heading: 5.1.2 0-1 loss linear classification theorem
text:
A hyperplane arrangement is a dissection of R D induced by a finite set of hyperplanes. At first glance, a hyperplane arrangement may appear to carry more structural information than a set of data points (a point configuration ). However, an effective approach to studying geometric structures involving points and hyperplanes is to examine the transformations between these two representations. By analyzing the duality between point configurations and hyperplane arrangements, it becomes evident that both possess equally rich combinatorial structures.
Although the space of all possible hyperplanes seems to exhibit infinite combinatorial complexity-since each hyperplane is defined by a continuous normal vector w k -the finiteness of data imposes strong constraints. Specifically, the number of distinct data partitions induced by hyperplanes is fi nite , which introduces an equivalence relation among hyperplanes.
In 1965, Cover provided a precise formula for counting the number of possible partitions induced by affine hyperplanes. Given N data points in general position, the number of linearly separable dichotomies is:
This result implies that the optimal 0-1 loss solution obtained by exhaustively searching over Cover's space S Cover is equivalent to the optimal solution obtained by searching the entire continuous space R D . Formally, given N data points in general position, we have

However, Cover's formula only counts the number of possible partitions; it does not provide an explicit method for enumerating the equivalence classes of hyperplanes. Here's a summary of the table's structure and the key information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into rows, each describing a different type of polynomial surface (or hypersurface) defined in Euclidean space.  Each row contains three columns:

*   **Polynomial Type:** This column lists the specific type of hypersurface being described (e.g., Axis-parallel hyperparralel, Affine Hyperplane, Hypersphere, Conic section, Arbitrary degree M polynomials).
*   **Embedded Data:** This column specifies the set of points that define the hypersurface within a given dimension *D*. These are the coordinates of the points that lie on the surface.
*   **Polynomial Expression:**  This column provides the polynomial equation that defines the hypersurface in terms of its embedded data. It shows how the coordinates (x₁, y₁, ..., z₁, ...) are related to each other based on the polynomial coefficients.
*   **Number of Data Required:** This column indicates the minimum number of points (*D*) needed to fully define the hypersurface.

**Key Information Represented:**

The table outlines the relationship between:

1.  **Hypersurface Type:** Different types of mathematical surfaces (like spheres, cones, planes) are listed.
2.  **Embedded Data Points:** The specific coordinates of points that lie on each surface are given.
3.  **Polynomial Equation:** A polynomial equation is provided to mathematically describe the hypersurface based on its embedded data. This equation allows you to calculate any point on the surface if you know its coordinates.
4.  **Data Requirements:** It specifies how many data points (*D*) are needed to uniquely define each type of hypersurface.

**In essence, the table provides a framework for understanding and representing polynomial surfaces in Euclidean space.** It shows how different types of surfaces can be defined using polynomials and how many points you need to fully describe them.

Based on the point-hyperplane duality, equivalence relations for linear classifiers on finite sets of data were established above. However, a linear classifier is often too restrictive in practice, as many problems require more complex decision boundaries. It is natural to ask whether it is possible to extend the theory to non-linear classification. This section explore a well-known concept in algebraic geometry, the M -tuple Veronese embedding , which allows the generalization of the previous strategy for solving classification problem with hyperplane classifier to problems involving hypersurface classifiers , with a worst-case time complexity O ( N G ) , where G = ( D + M D ) -1, and M is the degree of the polynomial defining the hypersurface. If both M and D are fixed constants, this again gives a polynomial-time algorithm for solving the 0-1 loss hypersurface classification problem.

M G be all monomials of degree M with variables x 0 , x 1 , . . . x D , where G ≤ ( D + M D ) -1. M G (¯ x )), we refer to the data list ρ M ( xs ) = [( M 0 (¯ x ) , M 1 (¯ x ) , . . . M G (¯ x )) | x ← xs ] as the embedded data list . The hyperplane classification over the embedded data lists ρ M ( xs ) is isomorphic to the polynomial hypersurface classification (defined by a degree M polynomial) over the original data list xs .
Proof First, we need to prove the existence of the hypersurface. For any G distinct points s in R D +1 , there exist a polynomial hypersurface S defined by a polynomial P with degree at most M such that s lies on S . heading: 5.1.4 Hypersurface decision trees are proper
text:
We now show that the hyperplane classification tree satisfies the axioms of a proper decision tree, as verified by the following lemma.
Lemma 26 Given a set of data xs and list of K hyperplanes hs K = [ h 1 , h 2 , . . . h K ] defined by normal vectors ws = [ w 1 , w 2 , . . . w K ] in R D +1 , where each hyperplane passes through exactly D data points. Define the positive and negative regions of each hyperplane as h + i = { x | x ∈ R D , w T i ¯ x ≥ 0 } and h -i = { x | x ∈ R D , w T i ¯ x < 0 } . Then, the decision tree constructed using these hyperplanes as splitting rules is a proper decision tree.
Proof Axioms 1 and 2 follow directly from the definition of h ± . Axioms 3 and 4 hold because, for any hyperplane h defined by a D -tuple of data points xs D we have
and
Thus axiom 3, 4 holds.
By the equivalence between hyperplane and hypersurface, as established in Theorem 24, it follows immediately that decision trees whose splitting rules are characterized by hypersurfaces passing through G -combinations of data points are also proper. We next show that the optimal decision tree constructed from these hypersurfaces, with G data points lying on them, is also optimal among all decision trees constructed from general hypersurfaces in R D .
Lemma 27 0-1 classification theorem for hypersurfaces decision tree model . Consider a data list xs of N data points of dimension D in general position, along with their associated labels. The optimal decision tree constructed using M -degree polynomial hypersurfaces, each passing through G points, achieves the same minimum 0-1 loss as the optimal decision tree constructed using arbitrary hypersurfaces in R D .
Proof We prove this by induction. The base case, involving a single linear hypersurface, has already been established in Theorem 25. For the inductive step, assume two subtrees t 1 and t 2 of size i and K -i -1, respectively, are optimal for the corresponding subregions xs + and xs -, determined by root h . We show that the tree t = DN ( t 1 , h, t 2 ) is optimal with respect to root h . Since t 1 and t 2 are optimal, any subtree of the same size constructed in xs + and xs -will have a equal or greater 0-1 loss than t 1 and t 2 . By definition of the objective function, we have
Thus, t = DN ( t 1 , h, t 2 ) is optimal for the fixed root h . Furthermore, for any arbitrary hypersurface defined by normal vector w ∈ R G , there exists a hypersurface passing through G points that induces the same partition xs + and xs -. Therefore, the optimal decision tree constructed using hypersurfaces through G points attains the same 0-1 loss as the optimal tree constructed from arbitrary hypersurfaces.
Although we assume that the data are in general position-which excludes axis-parallel hyperplanes, as they do not satisfy the general position assumption-the results also hold for axis-parallel hyperplanes, since they can be infinitesimally shifted to become hyperplanes in general position without affecting the predictions. Therefore, for axis-parallel hyperplane splitting rule, a single data point is required to characterize the hyperplane. By definition, an axis-parallel hyperplane, x i = c is uniquely determined by the i -th coordinate of any point lying on it. That is, for any point ( x 1 , x 2 , . . . , x D ) ∈ R D , the hyperplane parallel to the i -th axis passing through it is uniquely determined by x i . Since each data point has D coordinates, there are N × D possible axis-parallel splitting rules in total.

He (2025b) presents both divide-and-conquer (D&C) and sequential versions for the fused program of nestedCombs . While the D&C version is more easy to be parallelized, it complicates element ordering. Since ordering is critical in our applications-where combinations represent hyperplanes that encode essential predictive information-we adopt the sequential version of nestedCombs developed by He (2025b). One source of efficiency in nestedCombs is the representation of inner combinations as integers ranging from 1 to ( N G ) . Instead, their 'ranks' are used, allowing the information of each hyperplane to be indexed in O (1) time using the integer representation.

heading: 5.3.1 Complexity of size-constrained trees
text:
We present a theoretical analysis of the computational complexity of size-constrained decision trees. The complexity of depth-constrained trees can be upper-bounded by noting that a tree of depth d has at most 2 d +1 -1 splitting rules.
Complexity of the hyperplane decision tree problem The computational complexity of the optimal hypersurface decision tree problem is relatively straightforward to analyze. There are O ( N GK ) possible combinations of degree M hypersurfaces. The subprogram sodt has a worst-case complexity of O ( K !). Thus, solving the HODT problem requires
where O ( N G × D 3 × N ) represents the time required to compute hyperplane predictions. Although this complexity appears daunting, Part II demonstrates that the actual complexity of the hypersurface decision tree problem is significantly lower.
Furthermore, the average-case complexity of sodt rec is substantially lower than its theoretical upper bound. The worst-case complexity of sodt rec occurs only when the tree forms a 'chain' structure, which, as shown in the empirical analysis in Part II, is a rare occurrence.
Complexity of the axis-parallel decision tree problem For the axis-parallel decision tree (AODT) problem, there are O ( N × D ) possible splits defined by axis-parallel hyperplanes. Consequently, the complexity of the AODT problem is
Notably, when K splitting rules are fixed, both hypersurface and axis-parallel decision trees have a worst-case complexity bounded by O ( K !). However, the number of feasible trees in the hypersurface case is significantly smaller than in the axis-parallel case. This difference arises because, in axis-parallel decision trees, every pair of hyperplanes is mutually ancestral (a pair of hyperplanes is mutually ancestral if both h i ( ↙∨↘ ) h j and h j ( ↙∨↘ ) h i are viable, a case that rarely occurs in hypersurface decision trees).
Complexity of the decision tree problem over binary feature data The complexity of decision trees with binary feature data differs significantly. Due to axiom 4, if h 1 ↙ h 2 or h 1 ↘ h 2 , then h 1 ↘ h 2 or h 1 ↙ h 2 is impossible, i.e. if h 2 is in the left branch of h 1 , it cannot lie in the right branch of it.
In contrast, for binary feature data, hyperplanes represent the selection or non-selection of features, allowing any pair of branch nodes to be mutually ancestral and symmetric .
Symmetry implies that any branch node can serve as either the left or right child of the root. For example, when selecting features x i and x j , both x i ↙ x j and x i ↘ x j are possible when x i is the root, and x j ↙ x i and x j ↘ x i are possible with x j as root. This holds because, in all previous cases, hyperplanes are characterized by data points, and similarly, in the axis-parallel tree case, only one data item is needed to define each hyperplane, and this item always lies on one side of another hyperplane. However, for binary feature data, this method of characterization splitting rules as data points are no longer applicable. In the case of binary feature data, it is only necessary to consider whether a particular feature is selected or not and there are K features in total used to define decision trees of size K . Hence, for binary feature data, the number of possible decision trees depends not only on the shape of the tree (which can be counted using the Catalan number) but also the labels.
Therefore, assume D is much larger than K , the worst-case complexity for the decision tree problem with binary feature data is
where Catalan ( n ) is the Catalan number of n .

(2022)'s Quant-BnB algorithm, there is a function count remain 3D , which computes the combinatorial complexity of the search space, i.e., the number of remaining trees in each iteration. The initial call to count remain 3D , calculates this complexity directly from the input data without bounding parameters. For almost all datasets we have tested,

heading: 6 Conclusion
text:
The primary distinction between our research and other studies on ODT algorithms using combinatorial methods lies in philosophy: we argue that deriving optimal algorithms requires the highest level of rigor and clarity. Consequently, in this first of two papers, we introduce a generic algorithmic framework for deriving optimal algorithms directly from problem specifications. We begin by establishing a formal standard for designing optimal algorithms, identifying several key principles that highlight the level of rigor required for problem definitions in the study of optimal algorithms. These principles resemble the notion of a 'standard form' in the study of MIP solvers. Importantly, when a problem specification satisfies these principles, proving optimality becomes straightforward. This is analogous to general-purpose MIP solvers: once a problem is expressed in a standardized form, the solver can compute the solution directly, while the challenging part lies in producing the appropriate specification.
To demonstrate the importance and effectiveness of these standards, we present a concrete example by outlining the algorithmic and geometric foundations for solving the ODT problem. In the algorithmic section, we provide four generic and formal definitions of optimal decision tree problems using the 'proper decision tree' datatype, a recursive datatype with four associated axioms. Building on this, we develop four formal definitions of the ODT problem over arbitrary splitting rules and objective functions within a specified form.
Furthermore, these formulations yield four solutions to the ODT problem: we prove the existence of a dynamic programming solution for two and the non-existence of such a solution for the remaining two. To the best of our knowledge, these definitions and their corresponding solutions are both generic and novel.
Note that in this part, we have not yet presented a concrete implementation of any algorithm for solving optimal decision tree problems. Although most algorithms presented in this part are ready for implementation, a critical assumption remains unaddressed-the generation of the ancestry relation matrix K for a given K splitting rules. In the discussion of sodt rec , we assume K is pre-stored in memory; however, generating it actually requires an additional computational process. However, the construction of the ancestry relation matrix is problem-dependent, which is why we have not yet defined it, as our goal is to provide a generic solution rather than algorithms for special cases.
Indeed, in the second paper (Part II) of this series, one of the central focuses is to develop an incremental algorithm for efficiently constructing the ancestry relation matrix for hypersurface splitting rules, which lays the foundation for solving the hypersurface optimal decision tree problem. Building on the algorithmic and geometric foundations established here, Part II will present the first two algorithms designed to address optimal hypersurface decision tree problems.
Specifically, we focus on two algorithms, odt vec and odt rec -the former offering superior hardware compatibility, while the latter achieving stronger theoretical efficiency. We provide comprehensive experimental results analyzing the computational efficiency of odt vec and odt rec . Additionally, we conduct a thorough assessment of the generalization capabilities of hypersurface decision tree models and axis-parallel decision tree algorithms, including those learned by CART and Brita et al. (2025)'s ConTree algorithm, using both synthetic and real-world datasets.
